message:
test(ft-xbnl0.2.4): mid-flight cx-cancel HTTP snapshot (tick 380)

Added `distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang` —
snapshot test of what happens when cx-cancel fires WHILE a
DistributedHttpClient::get is in flight reading from a stalled
server.

Complements tick 313 (pre-cancel — cancel fires before get starts).
Together they pin two cancel timings:
- Pre-cancel: get() returns Err fast (tick 313 asserts is_err).
- Mid-flight cancel: get() does NOT observe the cancel mid-read.
  Elapsed hits the test's defensive 10s outer timeout (not the
  50ms cancel fire).

**Finding**: the distributed HTTP client's response-read path is
NOT cx-cancel-aware. This is the same class of gap as tick 328
documented for `timeout_with_cx` (budget observed, cancel not). A
hung distributed RPC call will eventually time out via its own
internal limits (if configured) but won't observe operator cancel.

Initial implementation had a bug — `let _ = listener.accept().await`
dropped the stream immediately, causing client to see UnexpectedEof
in ~9ms (which falsely looked like "cancel worked fast"). Fixed
to hold the accept result via `let _stream_hold`. With the proper
stall, the test now shows the real 10s-outer-timeout-fires behavior.

Test assertion is only "does not hang past 15s safety-net ceiling".
Prints observed elapsed + result to stderr so a future reader can
spot a behavior change (e.g. if asupersync tightens mid-flight
cancel observability).

Related: ft-kfkyi (closed) addressed the 3xx redirect security
concern. This mid-flight-cancel finding is lower-severity — not a
security issue, just an operational ergonomics gap — so recorded
as a snapshot test + doc rather than filed as a separate bead.

Test result: 1 passed; 0 failed (in 10.03s, dominated by the outer
timeout firing).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
